title: Sichuan Chilli Oil Noodles

ingredients.name: dried wheat noodles (hakka or any thin wheat noodles)

subs.name: any cooked pasta (spaghetti or vermicelli)
subs.notes: this sauce works on any noodle; use whatever is available

ingredients.name: dried red chillies, roughly crushed
visual_checks: use Kashmiri for colour with less heat, or guntur for serious fire
ingredients.notes: use Kashmiri for colour with less heat, or guntur for serious fire

subs.name: Korean gochugaru or Indian red chilli flakes
subs.notes: gochugaru gives a sweeter, more paprika-like heat; guntur chilli gives the most fire

subs.name: any neutral oil with a few drops of mustard oil
subs.notes: sesame oil is irreplaceable here for the authentic flavour — worth sourcing

ingredients.name: Sichuan peppercorns or black pepper, ground
peanuts, roasted and roughly crushed, for topping

subs.name: crushed fried peanuts or skip entirely
subs.notes: adds crunch and richness but the dish stands without them

steps.text: Place the crushed dried chillies and ground pepper in a heatproof bowl with the garlic and ginger. Heat the oil in a small saucepan until it just begins to smoke (around 180°C — it should shimmer and move quickly when you tilt the pan). Pour the hot oil slowly and carefully over the chilli mixture. It will sizzle aggressively. Stir and leave to infuse for 5 minutes.
Cook the noodles in a large pot of boiling water until just tender. Drain and rinse briefly with cold water to stop cooking. Toss with 1 teaspoon sesame oil and divide between bowls.
Make the dressing by combining the chilli oil (with all its infused bits), soy sauce, vinegar and sugar in a small bowl. Stir until the sugar dissolves.
Pour the dressing generously over the noodles in each bowl. Toss well so every strand is coated.

Top with sliced spring onions, sesame seeds and crushed peanuts if using. Serve immediately, or leave at room temperature for up to 30 minutes — the noodles absorb the sauce and become even more flavourful.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: noodles → any cooked pasta (spaghetti or vermicelli) (this sauce works on any noodle; use whatever is available); dried-red-chilli → Korean gochugaru or Indian red chilli flakes (gochugaru gives a sweeter, more paprika-like heat; guntur chilli gives the most fire); peanut → crushed fried peanuts or skip entirely (adds crunch and richness but the dish stands without them); gingelly-oil → any neutral oil with a few drops of mustard oil (sesame oil is irreplaceable here for the authentic flavour — worth sourcing)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: This is one of the most India-friendly Chinese recipes because most ingredients are pantry staples. Kashmiri red chillies give a beautiful deep-red chilli oil with moderate heat; guntur chillies make it very hot. Gingelly oil (nallennai) is your sesame oil. Hakka noodles are the go-to choice. Making the chilli oil from scratch takes 5 minutes and is infinitely better than any bottled alternative.
